title: A Guide To Stablecoins: Majority Fiat-Backed Stablecoins—USDT, USDC, PYUSD
gist: ARK's deep-dive on USDC, USDT, and PYUSD shows the GENIUS Act cements a Circle/Tether duopoly by making majority fiat-backed the only eligible US payment stablecoin, while custody structure (Paxos bankruptcy-remote vs. Circle's BlackRock money-market fund vs. Tether's undisclosed non-fiat custodians) and the October 10, 2025 liquidation event reveal that peg stability depends as much on exchange microstructure as on reserves.

themes.theme: GENIUS Act reshapes the competitive landscape

insights.claim: The GENIUS Act makes majority fiat-backed stablecoins the only type eligible for issuers to offer as payment stablecoins in the US, legally entrenching the incumbent model over multi-collateral and synthetic-dollar alternatives.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Regulatory design, not just market competition, locks in the fiat-backed model as the only compliant US path — favors incumbents Circle/Tether and forces new entrants into this structure.
insights.locator: Now that Congress has passed the GENIUS Act

insights.claim: Majority fiat-backed stablecoins account for more than 85% of the $313 billion total stablecoin supply, and the segment is a duopoly dominated by Circle's USDC and Tether's USDT that dwarfs all competitors including PayPal's PYUSD.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Total addressable stablecoin market is overwhelmingly concentrated in one structural type and two issuers — new entrants face a scale wall, not just a product-quality wall.
insights.locator: The most popular stablecoin type, majority-fiat backed stablecoins

insights.claim: The GENIUS Act's Section 4(a) bars stablecoin issuers from passing Treasury-reserve yield directly to token holders, a securities-law-driven restriction the author flags as still contested in ongoing crypto market-structure legislation.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: If this restriction is loosened in future legislation, issuers could compete directly on yield, which would upend current economics that instead rely on partner programs like Coinbase's rewards.
insights.locator: Historically, stablecoin issuers have not passed the yield

insights.claim: Circle is one of several firms pursuing a federal bank charter (with OCC conditional approval already granted), while Anchorage Digital is positioned as the only OCC-licensed crypto bank and will issue Tether's new US-focused USAT stablecoin.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Bank-charter status is becoming a competitive moat in stablecoin issuance — watch OCC charter approvals as a leading indicator of which issuers gain lower custodian/counterparty friction.
insights.locator: Anchorage Digital Bank will issue USAT

themes.theme: Reserve composition reflects strategic and jurisdictional divergence

insights.claim: Circle and PayPal (PYUSD) hold effectively all reserves in USD cash and cash equivalents (repo agreements, short-term T-bills), while Tether backs USDT with only ~76% fiat-based assets and ~24% in bitcoin, precious metals, secured loans, and other investments, enabled by its El Salvador Digital Asset Service Provider license giving it more reserve latitude than US/EU issuers.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Tether carries embedded bitcoin/commodity price risk in its reserve base that USDC/PYUSD holders don't bear — a structural risk premium investors should price into USDT's perceived safety versus its peers.
insights.locator: Tether has backed USDT with the following reserves

insights.claim: Tether's forthcoming US-focused stablecoin, USAT, must comply with GENIUS Act reserve requirements — unlike USDT itself — creating a bifurcated Tether product line: an offshore, reserve-flexible USDT and a US-compliant, cash/Treasury-only USAT.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Tether is hedging regulatory risk by running two parallel stablecoins rather than converting USDT itself — signals it expects to keep USDT's higher-yield offshore reserve mix rather than dilute it for US compliance.
insights.locator: Reserves backing USAT, its recently launched US focused stablecoin

insights.claim: Approximately 46% of USDT supply sits on the Tron blockchain, which dominates stablecoin usage in Latin America, Africa, and Asia, giving Tether a structural distribution advantage in emerging markets that USDC and PYUSD lack.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Tether's emerging-market/Tron dominance is a moat that's hard for US-centric USDC/PYUSD to replicate given their regulatory posture — relevant for anyone assessing emerging-market crypto payment flows.
insights.locator: Tether has been focused on the rest of the world

themes.theme: Custody architecture, not just reserve type, drives real durability

insights.claim: Paxos places PYUSD reserves in segregated, bankruptcy-remote accounts at qualified institutions, offering the strongest legal protection to holders — even in the event of Paxos's own hypothetical bankruptcy — versus Circle's and Tether's structures.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: On pure holder-protection grounds PYUSD's custody design is arguably superior to USDC/USDT despite its much smaller scale — a structural argument for PYUSD adoption that isn't about liquidity or brand.
insights.locator: Placing all assets in segregated, bankruptcy-remote accounts

insights.claim: Circle holds the majority of USDC reserves in a BlackRock-managed 2a-7 government money market fund, which segregates reserves from Circle's balance sheet but does not offer the same bankruptcy-remote guarantees as Paxos and introduces indirect exposure to BlackRock as a counterparty.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: USDC's reserve safety is partly a bet on BlackRock's fund structure, not just on Circle — a hidden counterparty dependency investors evaluating USDC's 'safety' should factor in.
insights.locator: Circle holds the majority of USDC reserves in a Blackrock

insights.claim: Tether is the only one of the three issuers with undisclosed custodians and storage methods for its non-fiat reserves (bitcoin, precious metals, secured loans), a transparency gap that persists despite Tether's regular attestations on its fiat holdings.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: The opacity is concentrated exactly in the ~24% non-fiat sleeve of Tether's reserves — the part of USDT backing that's hardest to independently verify, a key due-diligence flag for large USDT holders.
insights.locator: Tether's is the exception, with undisclosed custodians

themes.theme: The October 10, 2025 liquidation event as a live stress test

insights.claim: During the October 10, 2025 mass liquidation event ('10/10'), USDC depegged by a few cents on Binance for a short period not because of reserve impairment but due to exchange-specific order-book pricing, thin platform liquidity, and API outages that disrupted mint/redeem arbitrage.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Peg stability isn't purely a reserve-quality question — exchange market structure can cause real (if temporary) depegs even for well-reserved stablecoins, meaning venue selection matters for large stablecoin holders during stress events.
insights.locator: such as the recent liquidation event on October 10, 2025

insights.claim: During 10/10, the derivatives exchange dYdX went offline for eight hours and Lighter experienced a 4.5-hour outage, showing that perpetual-futures infrastructure failures can cascade into stablecoin peg instability during extreme volatility.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Derivatives-venue uptime is now a systemic risk factor for stablecoin pegs, not just a trading-venue issue — worth monitoring exchange infrastructure resilience as a leading indicator of stress contagion.
insights.locator: Market-wide infrastructure failures followed

insights.claim: During 10/10, USDC holders on Binance fled into USDT seeking refuge, and this substitution itself added further sell pressure on USDC, meaning stablecoin-to-stablecoin flight can be self-reinforcing and destabilizing rather than neutral.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: In a two-horse stablecoin market, a flight-to-safety flow between USDC and USDT can itself be a depeg mechanism — a reflexive dynamic worth watching in future volatility spikes.
insights.locator: This problem was exacerbated by USDC holders on Binance

insights.claim: USDT remained resilient during 10/10 specifically because of its deeper liquidity on Binance, not because of superior reserve backing — implying USDT's relative stability during that event was a liquidity artifact rather than a fundamentals signal.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Don't read USDT's steadier peg during 10/10 as proof of superior reserve quality — it reflects exchange-specific liquidity depth, a distinction that matters for comparing issuer risk.
insights.locator: remained resilient due to its deeper liquidity on the platform

themes.theme: Mint/redeem fee structures signal go-to-market strategy

insights.claim: Paxos charges no redemption fee for PYUSD conversions, an explicit strategy to drive adoption, liquidity, and platform integrations against the entrenched USDC/USDT duopoly.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Zero redemption fees are a subsidized customer-acquisition cost for PYUSD — a sign Paxos/PayPal are prioritizing market-share growth over near-term fee revenue.
insights.locator: Paxos is trying to drive adoption, liquidity, and platform integrations

insights.claim: Tether charges a flat fee (greater of ~$1,000 or 0.1%) with a $100,000 minimum redemption, while Circle uses a tiered redemption fee schedule (0% to 0.05% depending on volume tier) — reflecting differentiated institutional versus developer-focused go-to-market approaches.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Fee structure differences show Circle optimizing for a broad developer/fintech base while Tether optimizes for high-minimum institutional flow — relevant to who each issuer is really building for.
insights.locator: Circle, for example, has a tiered redemption fee system

insights.claim: Retail users cannot mint or redeem any of USDC, USDT, or PYUSD directly with the issuer — only KYC-approved whitelisted institutions can, meaning the arbitrage mechanism that stabilizes the peg is structurally inaccessible to retail holders.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Peg stability for retail holders is entirely dependent on institutional arbitrageurs and secondary-market liquidity, not on any direct redemption right — a key risk disclosure point often glossed over.
insights.locator: Retail users cannot mint or redeem directly

themes.theme: Compliance and freeze behavior reveal political economy, not just jurisdiction

insights.claim: Regulatory domicile does not predict enforcement cooperation: offshore-based Tether froze roughly 30x more USDT value than US-regulated Circle froze USDC between 2023 and 2025 — about $3.3 billion versus $109 million.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Investors assuming offshore issuers offer more censorship resistance should note Tether actually freezes far more assets than the US-domiciled Circle — sovereignty risk doesn't map cleanly to issuer jurisdiction.
insights.locator: Tether froze ~30x more USDT between 2023 and 2025 than Circle did USDC

insights.claim: 38% of Tether's blacklisted USDT addresses were handled in direct coordination with US law enforcement despite Tether being headquartered and licensed in El Salvador, indicating substantial de facto cooperation with US regulators regardless of formal offshore status.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Offshore stablecoin licensing doesn't insulate holders from US enforcement reach in practice — relevant for anyone using USDT as a censorship-resistance play.
insights.locator: 38% of blacklisted addresses were handled in coordination

themes.theme: Distribution moat: chains, exchanges, and PYUSD's stalled traction

insights.claim: USDC has native issuance on 32 blockchains versus USDT's 12 and PYUSD's just 4, giving Circle by far the broadest technical distribution footprint even though USDT and PYUSD compensate via LayerZero's OFT for cross-chain functionality.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Circle's native multichain integration is a durable moat that's costly for competitors to replicate chain-by-chain — a structural advantage independent of reserve quality or fees.
insights.locator: USDC is the most widely available by network integration

insights.claim: USDT dominates the Asian centralized exchanges (Binance, OKX, ByBit) while USDC dominates on Coinbase and Kraken, reflecting a geographic/regulatory split in exchange-level stablecoin liquidity.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Traders' effective stablecoin choice is largely dictated by which exchange they use, not by independent preference — a structural liquidity lock-in that protects incumbents' regional strongholds.
insights.locator: USDT dominates the Asian CEXs

insights.claim: PYUSD has 'not hit escape velocity,' surpassing the $1 billion supply mark only on Ethereum and fluctuating above and below $1 billion on Solana, despite PayPal's consumer brand and multi-chain integration.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Brand recognition alone (PayPal) has not been sufficient to break the USDC/USDT duopoly — a cautionary data point for any new stablecoin entrant relying on consumer brand rather than DeFi/exchange integration depth.
insights.quote: which has not hit escape velocity, has surpassed the billion-dollar mark only on Ethereum
insights.locator: PYUSD, which has not hit escape velocity

themes.theme: Yield restrictions and the workaround economy

insights.claim: Because issuers cannot legally pass Treasury-reserve yield to holders, Coinbase's rewards program — paying users roughly 3.5% to hold USDC on its platform — functions as an indirect, partner-layer workaround to the GENIUS Act's yield-sharing restriction.
insights.speaker: ARK Invest Research
insights.so_what: Yield competition in stablecoins is shifting to the exchange/platform layer rather than the issuer layer — investors should watch platform reward programs, not issuer yield policy, as the real competitive battleground.
insights.locator: Coinbase's rewards program, which pays users ~3.5%
